narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've poured into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—landing at a 49% chance of not passing. Picture your story through the model's eyes: it started with your Year 3 grades placing you in the 2nd decile at school, which stood out as the biggest red flag, whispering that the rigors of upper-level law might prove an uphill battle even if you pushed hard. Layered on that was your upper-middle family income, which oddly enough nudged the outlook toward struggle, perhaps because it didn't signal the extra safety nets that sometimes help others weather tough academic storms. Your Year 1 decile of 1st didn't help either, painting an early picture of challenges settling in from the start. Then came your LSAT score of 31 and undergrad GPA of 3.5—solid steps, but in this mix, they tilted further toward caution, like sturdy but not standout foundations for the bar's intense demands. Even factors like being a white female pursuing full-time studies wove in subtly, each pulling the needle a bit more toward that worrisome 49%. It's not that any one piece defined you, but together they sketched a profile where the model foresaw hurdles outweighing the strengths, especially with your clear aim for law as your target. This isn't the end of your story—many have rewritten predictions like this with targeted prep—but it explains why we had to draw the line here. You've got resilience in your corner; channel it wisely.
